evidence_quotes.quote: Companies like Adobe keep their customers trough dark patterns.
Microsoft is also once again doing these things by asking you every couple days if you changed your mind.
No, please, for the 50th time, I do not want Edge as my default browser and neither do I want you to just change it.
Games are software, they contain dark patterns like every other type of software and EA and Ubisoft are just as horrible as Adobe and Microsoft in their own special ways.
one thing I see way too much these days is companies making you click 50 check boxes for each data processor, to get them all to stop processing your data.
Having to pay an early cancellation fee is possibly one of the worst practices a monopolistic company use to keep their customers from switching.
Please we need one technical standard. A flag somewhere in the HTTP request to tell companies we don't want their cookies or tracking.
We had such a thing, it was called the Do-Not-Track Header and companies threw it out because it would spit in their tracking marketing soup (if they implemented it at all).
advertisers want to target their customers properly but but all the data collection just causes giant companies to get targeted by hackers.

paragraphs.text: Companies like Adobe keep their customers trough dark patterns. Having to pay an early cancellation fee is possibly one of the worst practices a monopolistic company use to keep their customers from switching. Microsoft is also once again doing these things by asking you every couple days if you changed your mind. No, please, for the 50th time, I do not want Edge as my default browser and neither do I want you to just change it. Imagine someone who's creepy asks to have sex with you and every time you say no they just ask you every 3 days again and again. No means no. Same goes for Windows. By asking users to log into their Microsoft account you automatically bind them to a service instead of providing alternatives at all. Remember what happened in the browser wars in the 90s? Exactly the same is now happening with their Cloud Service OneDrive and Microsoft is just about as annoying as they are with their browser. I would also kindly ask to have a look at Stop Killing Games. Games are software, they contain dark patterns like every other type of software and EA and Ubisoft are just as horrible as Adobe and Microsoft in their own special ways. Microsoft as a game company is a prime example of buying up random game studios, merging them, just to lay off the staff half a year later. This way they keep fastening their monopoly position in the market. I work as a web developer and one thing I see way too much these days is companies making you click 50 check boxes for each data processor, to get them all to stop processing your data. Please we need one technical standard. A flag somewhere in the HTTP request to tell companies we don't want their cookies or tracking. We had such a thing, it was called the Do-Not-Track Header and companies threw it out because it would spit in their tracking marketing soup (if they implemented it at all). I can understand that advertisers want to target their customers properly but but all the data collection just causes giant companies to get targeted by hackers. If people want to own their data it needs to exist somewhere locally or on a server they actually trust, and not one that is thrust upon them because there is no alternative for them to go to. Especially when you look at things like social media none of them implement open protocls enough for people to easily migrate their posts and other content to different websites. Neither is it easily possible to access content from one social media on other social media. If Alphabet (Facebook) can implement ActivityPub on Threads so can they on Instagram and Facebook. Every other company can do it too. If E-Mail works as a system, ActivityPub will work too. I really hope this reaches the right people and makes a change in the industry. Thank you for reading and feel free to contact me if you need more information on these things.
